Original_Search_Title: Personalities of the Year 2008
Headline: Liberia: Personalities of the Year 2008
Body_Text:
In keeping with its tradition, the INQUIRER Newspaper has selected and recognized several personalities and institutions for their exemplary roles in the year 2008.
This exercise has been based on the invaluable contributions these selected individuals and institutions have made over the one year for the good of humanity and our common patrimony, Liberia.

Original_Search_Title: Tough Time Awaits Kwara Residents, Says Saraki
Headline: Nigeria: Tough Time Awaits Kwara Residents, Says Saraki
Body_Text:
Ilorin — THE Governor of Kwara State, Dr Bukola Saraki, has urged the people of the state to brace up for hard times next year by making use of every opportunity that would be provided by the government to expand the economy, saying the new year would be very tough in the state.
Gov. Saraki, who spoke with newsmen in an interview in Ilorin shortly after he announced a budget proposal of N72.2 billion for the next fiscal year, also pleaded with the Federal Government to enforce the implementation of the cassava five percent on all the factories in order to create more jobs for Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: Climate Change Threatens Livelihoods
Headline: Africa: Climate Change Threatens Livelihoods
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Climate change will affect the Zambezi River basin more severely than any other river system in the world, according to Kenneth Msibi, Water Policy and Strategy Expert for the Southern African Development Community (SADC).
Increased floods, drought and increased levels of disease threaten lives and livelihoods all along the river's length.

Original_Search_Title: Sylva's Wife Identifies With Odi Widows
Headline: Nigeria: Sylva's Wife Identifies With Odi Widows
Body_Text:
Wife of the Bayelsa State governor, Mrs. Alanyingi Sylva, yesterday, identified with the widows of Odi, many of whom lost their husbands during the invasion of the community by federal troops, calling on women to always take the lead and act as peace makers.
Mrs. Sylva gave the charge at Odi, Kolokuma/Opokuma local government area of the state during her visit to about 160 widows at the town hall.

Original_Search_Title: Edo Napep Disburses N60.5 Million
Headline: Nigeria: Edo Napep Disburses N60.5 Million
Body_Text:
Edo State Co-ordinator of the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP), Mrs. Patricia Imade, weekend in Benin City, disclosed that over N60.5m has been disbursed to some communities and cooperative societies in the state under its first and second phase of its NAPEP Village Solution Scheme.
Speaking at the disbursement of N14.5m to five communities across the state under the third phase of the Programme, Mrs. Imade said that 15 communities and 61 co-operative societies benefited during the first and second phase as they were given micro credit for the execution of their projects.

Original_Search_Title: 'Vision 2020 Requires $4,800 PCI, More Entrepreneurs'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Vision 2020 Requires $4,800 PCI, More Entrepreneurs'
Body_Text:
Lagos — To join the league of largest economies in 2020, less than 12 years from now, Nigeria must attain a Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of $960 billion with a Per Capita Income (PCI) of about $4,800.
The submission was made by a financial expert, Mr. Adebiyi Adesuyi, who affirmed in a statement that the present size of the nation's economy is $160 billion with a PCI of $1,100.

Original_Search_Title: Training Centre to Kick Off in 2009
Headline: Angola: Training Centre to Kick Off in 2009
Body_Text:
Luanda — The Research and Training Centre in Rural Extension of Agriculture Ministry, in northern Malanje province, whose inauguration was initially set for this year, will only start operating in 2009, after the inclusion of necessary elements.
Speaking to Angop Monday in Luanda, António Castame, the centre's technician, said that for the full functioning of the structure it is necessary to equip the laboratories with reagents and wasteful materials, as well as assembling of green-house and installing the irrigation system micro-spraying.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Enters N46 Billion Pact On Rice Production
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Enters N46 Billion Pact On Rice Production
Body_Text:
The Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) has entered into a 25-year pact with a private investor to boost rice production.
The NDDC's Managing Director, Mr Timi Alaibe, told newsmen on Wednesday in Port Harcourt that the Commission would pay 20 per cent of the money, while the private investor, Agritech Nigeria Limited, would pay 80 per cent.

Original_Search_Title: Energy Crisis Deepens
Headline: Uganda: Energy Crisis Deepens
Body_Text:
Kampala — MOST Kampala residents have shifted from using electricity to charcoal, previously regarded as a cheap source of energy for the poor. But the charcoal prices have doubled within less than a year . Further increases are predicted if nothing is done to put things right. Gerald Tenywa tells how the masses are coping.
AISHA Kamara, a resident of Kinawataka, a Kampala suburb, leads a life full of challenges. "We have food, but no charcoal to cook with," says the 33-year-old Kamara. "So we look for wood chips and gather sawdust, which we use to cook."

Original_Search_Title: Rediscovering the Art of Adire
Headline: Nigeria: Rediscovering the Art of Adire
Body_Text:
Wives of Foreign Heads of Mission to Nigeria recently paid a courtesy call to the Nike Art Gallery in Piwoyi, Abuja. Leading the delegation was wife of the Egyptian Ambassador, Mrs. Heba Naguib who explained the need for the visit. She said, "This is in a bid to further bind the diplomatic community to Nigeria culture and art wise. Cultural integration is key for us wherever it is we find ourselves. This is also a very good opportunity to educate us on Nigerian cultural values and art."
Going around the gallery showcasing works of art, being described by the gallery boss, Chief Nike Okundaye, the group expressed great admiration and awe for the pieces on display. Commending how well the paintings, pencil/charcoal, beads and most of all the adire pieces reflected the beauty and fullness of Nigeria and Africa as a whole, Mrs. Adriana Baistrocchi said, "Being in Africa over a long time now, these works are simply fabulous and mind blowing."

Original_Search_Title: ENCISS, World Bank Reunite a Divided Land
Headline: Sierra Leone: ENCISS, World Bank Reunite a Divided Land
Body_Text:
The River Jong estuary that runs through Bonthe District seemed calmed and serene as the orange glow of the evening sun disappears beneath the horizon. The estuary, festooned with mangrove swamps, provides a natural and peaceful habitat to more than a hundred species of birds and other marine life that have co-existed through the ages.
The boat ride on the serene waters on to the island was as expected, equally calm. The tranquility of Bonthe Island, free from the usual hustle and bustle of modern cities provides an ideal holiday spot. Descendants and non-descendants alike use to flock to the Island to spend their Christmas holidays that has earned the name "Christmas Island". That was long ago.

Original_Search_Title: Inside Joseph Kony's Camp Swahili
Headline: Uganda: Inside Joseph Kony's Camp Swahili
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE UPDF commandos on Tuesday evening matched into the deserted LRA camps in the Garamba jungles in north-east DR Congo that were bombarded by the airforce on Sunday.
The Special Forces that were air-dropped five kilometres from the main camp called Camp Shahili, picked their way in the dense, swampy forest infested with wild animals.

Original_Search_Title: Disease-Resistant Cassava Cloned at Namulonge
Headline: Uganda: Disease-Resistant Cassava Cloned at Namulonge
Body_Text:
Kampala — SCIENTISTS at the National Crop Resources Research Institute in Namulonge, Wakiso district have developed cassava clones resistant to cassava brown streak disease.
The clones were developed from 20,000 cassava seeds, which were imported from Tanzania early this year. "So far out of the 116 clones, 15 may be tolerant to the disease," said Dr. Titus Alicai, a cassava scientist at Namulonge.

Original_Search_Title: Over to You, Chief Justice
Headline: Ghana: Over to You, CJ
Body_Text:
The public Agenda newspaper reported last week Friday that a magistrate Court at Akim Oda, in the Birim Central Municipality had sentenced a 72 year old farmer, Kwadwo Nkansah to six months imprisonment with hard labour, for stealing a bunch of plantain from one Albert Akwasi Amoako's farm at Akim Swedru. According to the story, the accused pleaded guilty to the charge and was accordingly sentenced by the court.
Though the judge based his judgement on the relevant laws of the land, The Chronicle thinks the judgment handed down to the 72 year old man was very harsh. The story did not state whether the accused was a first or second time offender of the same crime, but no matter the situation, we at The Chronicle think a caution and subsequent discharge would have been enough, instead of being thrown into jail at a ripe age of 72, after being charged with such a minor offence.

Original_Search_Title: NDDC Predicts Bumper Rice Harvest
Headline: Nigeria: NDDC Predicts Bumper Rice Harvest
Body_Text:
Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) has predicted a bumper harvest in rice farming next year.
Aslo the Chairman of Isoko South Local Government Council, Delta State, Chief Askia Ogieh has empowered six hundred and sixty persons with Agro-Equipment and other farming tools as starter packs in his effort to boost food production in the council area.

Original_Search_Title: Food Producers Have Many Opportunities to Benefit From Current High Food Prices
Headline: Uganda: Food Producers Have Many Opportunities to Benefit From Current High Food Prices
Body_Text:
Kampala — ONE of the challenges of market liberalisation in Africa is the ability of local farmers to actually access global markets.
Despite the current rise in food prices worldwide, backed by rising incomes in emerging economies, especially Asia, agricultural markets in Africa are still inefficient This makes it difficult for farmers to benefit from current global market trends.

Original_Search_Title: Hailstorm Hits Mbarara
Headline: Uganda: Hailstorm Hits Mbarara
Body_Text:
Kampala — Over 45,000 residents from 200 homesteads in Biharwe, Mbarara, were hit by hailstorm on Tuesday, which destroyed their crops and houses.
Biharwe NRM chairman, Mukasa Kamwebaze, told Saturday Vision that Migamba, Nyakinengo, Rwagaaju and Nile Rugarama were affected. He said the affected crops included bananas, cassava, millet and maize.

Original_Search_Title: AGIP to Partner FG On Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: AGIP to Partner FG On Agriculture
Body_Text:
AGIP is to partner with the Federal Government to enhance the quality of farming in the country. General Manager of the multi-national oil firm, Mr Filbrizo Trilli who said this at Obie, Onelga Local Government Area of Rivers State said the firm would assist rural farmers with modern farming technology.
The MD who spoke at the annual green river project farmers' day celebration said the firm had signed an MoU with the National Centre for Agricultural Mechanization (NCAM) to produce suitable machines that would be tailored to meet the local needs of the rural farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis Requires Action
Headline: Zambia: Food Crisis Requires Action
Body_Text:
IT is only on the continents of Africa and Asia that even as we grapple with the food security situation calls by authorities encouraging people to embrace crop diversification have fallen on deaf ears.
Unfortunately so, it is from these two vast continents that reports of food shortages have been registered with famine and its effects afflicting millions of its inhabitants.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Karwor Named Country's Best Rice Farmer 2008
Headline: Liberia: Chief Karwor Named Country's Best Rice Farmer 2008
Body_Text:
President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf has lauded Liberian farmers for complementing government's efforts in ensuring that enough food is available on the Liberian market at an affordable rate.
The President made particular mention of the participation of farmers in this year's National Agricultural Fair in Voinjama, Lofa County.

Original_Search_Title: Choosing an Endless Path to Literacy
Headline: Uganda: Choosing an Endless Path to Literacy
Body_Text:
Kampala — You may fail to recognise him when he is seated in class with his classmates.
But his big and rough jaws due to years of shaving, broad shoulders and muscled arms, betray his age, making him stand out among his Senior One classmates.

Original_Search_Title: President  Johnson-Sirleaf Wins FAO  Award
Headline: Liberia: President  Johnson-Sirleaf Wins FAO  Award
Body_Text:
President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf has been awarded the 2008 Food and Agricultural Organization's Ceres Medal. The award was presented to the President on Saturday at the National Agricultural Fair held this year in Voinjama, Lofa County.
The Ceres Medal is awarded to distinguished women who have made an outstanding contribution to social and humanitarian affairs or to agricultural development and food security.

Original_Search_Title: "We will Promote Investments in Country," says Nigerian Higher Commissioner
Headline: Sierra Leone: "We will Promote Investments in Country," says Nigerian Higher Commissioner
Body_Text:
The newly appointed Nigerian High Commissioner to Sierra Leone Godson Echegile is a banker-turned-politician. Despite his busy diplomatic schedule, he found time to talk to ADEZE OJUKWU, media coordinator Nigerian National Union in Freetown on critical issues about the region.
His Excellency Sir What are your major goals as Ambassador to Sierra Leone?

Original_Search_Title: Chief Zanzan Karwor NamedBest/Most Productive Rice Farmer for 2008
Headline: Liberia: Chief Zanzan Karwor NamedBest/Most Productive Rice Farmer for 2008
Body_Text:
Voinjama — President Ellen Johnson has lauded Liberian farmers for complementing government's efforts to ensure that enough food is available on the Liberian market at an affordable rate. The President made particular mention of the participation of farmers throughout this year's National Agricultural Fair in Voinjama, Lofa County.
According to an Executive Mansion release, the Liberian leader congratulated the winners of this year's awards for increased food production and other agricultural sectors. The President urged farmers throughout the country not to relent in their drive to increase food production in the country.

Original_Search_Title: President Johnson Sirleaf Wins FAO Award
Headline: Liberia: President Johnson Sirleaf Wins FAO Award
Body_Text:
Voinjama — President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf has been awarded the 2008 Food and Agricultural Organization's Ceres Medal.
The award was presented to the President on Saturday at the National Agricultural Fair held this year in Voinjama, Lofa County. The Ceres Medal is awarded to distinguished women who have made an outstanding contribution to social and humanitarian affairs or to agricultural development and food security.

Original_Search_Title: Feeding the Continent - Meeting the Challenge With a Green Revolution
Headline: Africa: Feeding the Continent - Meeting the Challenge With a Green Revolution
Body_Text:
"Africa is the only region of the world that is not going to be able to eradicate extreme poverty and hunger if we continue with business as usual" - Vice President of the Alliance for a Green Revolution
An impassioned call for a uniquely African Green Revolution that is specifically geared to meeting the region's particular agroecological conditions and the needs of its peoples was recently made by Dr Akinwunmi Adesina at the Commonwealth's headquarters.

Original_Search_Title: Oshiomhole Visits Rainstorm Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Oshiomhole Visits Rainstorm Victims
Body_Text:
Edo State Governor, Comrade Adams Oshiomhole weekend commiserated with victims of rainstorm disaster at Ugbegun-Nebudin in Esan Central Local Government Area of Edo State and promised to send relief materials to them to ameloirate their hardship.
Vanguard recalls that while Nigerians were celebrating Oshiomhole victory's on November 11, 2008, the people of Ugbegun-Nebudin were in tears as rainstorm destroyed 37 houses.

Original_Search_Title: Blue Revolution, The Next Step In The Fight Against Hunger
Headline: Mozambique: Blue Revolution, The Next Step In The Fight Against Hunger
Body_Text:
Maputo — <!--  /* Style Definitions */ p.MsoNormal, li.MsoNormal, div.MsoNormal 	{mso-style-parent:""; 	margin:0cm; 	margin-bottom:.0001pt; 	mso-pagination:widow-orphan; 	font-size:12.0pt; 	font-family:"Times New Roman"; 	mso-fareast-font-family:"Times New Roman";} @page Section1 	{size:612.0pt 792.0pt; 	margin:72.0pt 90.0pt 72.0pt 90.0pt; 	mso-header-margin:36.0pt; 	mso-footer-margin:36.0pt; 	mso-paper-source:0;} div.Section1 	{page:Section1;} -->
African countries, including Mozambique, should consider the importance of "Blue Revolution" for mass production of fish by using simple techniques, for hunger eradication and improve people's diet.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava - IITA Develops Cost Efficient Flash Dryer
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava - IITA Develops Cost Efficient Flash Dryer
Body_Text:
The International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) has developed cost effective and efficient flash dryer that produces 300% more cassava flour while using 40% less fuel.
Cassava farmers and processors are set to earn bigger profits and get better-quality product from this new technology developed by scientists working at the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture and national partners in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Government Receives U.S.$ 5 Million Grant From AfDB
Headline: Rwanda: Government Receives U.S.$ 5 Million Grant From AfDB
Body_Text:
Kigali — The African Development Bank (AfDB) has donated US$ 5m to the government to boost food production for this year's final agricultural season in the country.
The grant agreement was signed yesterday at the offices of the Ministry of Finance and Economic Planning (MINICOFIN) in Kigali City between the Finance Minister James Musoni and Diko Jacob Mukete, the Resident Representative of the continental bank.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Fails to Curb Food Inflation
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Government Moves to Curb Food Inflation 'Taking Time'
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — Government measures aimed at reducing the cost of staple commodities in the Republic of Congo, where salaries have remained unchanged for years, have failed to stem food inflation. Many people have been forced to change their diets, which has had an adverse impact on health.
"It is getting harder to eat," Ernest Mbou, a local teacher, who heads a family of eight, told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: No Credit for Food Crops
Headline: Mozambique: No Credit for Food Crops
Body_Text:
Maputo — Almost no Mozambican farmers producing food crops have access to credit, according to Fernando Songane, coordinator of the government's National Agricultural Development Programme (PROAGRI).
Addressing a Maputo workshop on "Overcoming the Challenges of Agriculture in Africa" on Tuesday, Songane said that, apart from a few vegetable producers, "we can say that no producer of food crops - such as maize or cassava - is able to obtain credit".

Original_Search_Title: Son Endorses Imoke's Cassava Flour Project
Headline: Nigeria: Son Endorses Imoke's Cassava Flour Project
Body_Text:
Calabar — The Standards Orgainsation of Nigeria (SON) has issued a seal of acceptance to cassava flour packaged by the Partnership Opportunities for Women Empowerment Realisation (POWER), the pet project of the wife of the governor of Cross River State, Mrs. Obioma Liyel-Imoke.
The product was packaged from the cassava processing centre of the organisation at Okpodu in Yala Local Government Area of Cross River state.

Original_Search_Title: UN to Invest $50 Million in Cassava Processing
Headline: Nigeria: UN to Invest $50 Million in Cassava Processing
Body_Text:
Abuja — International Fund for Agriculture Development (IFAD) has revealed plans to invest $50 million to improve the processing of cassava in the country and increase market accessibility of the product.
Furthermore, plans are underway to diversify the huge focus on cassava and spotlight other crops that would better the lives and livelihood of small scale rural farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Block Aid to Northern Region
Headline: Uganda: Floods Block Aid to Northern Region
Body_Text:
Kampala — POOR roads resulting from floods have hampered humanitarian access to the north and northeastern Uganda.
The United Nations Office for Humanitarian Affairs, in a report issued on Monday, said the Pader-Puranga road and the Lira-Kitgum road had deteriorated.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Earmarks N12 Billion for Rice Farming
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Earmarks N12 Billion for Rice Farming
Body_Text:
Asaba — Delta State Government has set aside N12 billion to kick start an integrated rice farm that seeks to produce and process mainly table rice for onshore and offshore market as well as to cushion the effect of global economic meltdown.
The project, which starts next month, which has provision for the production of cross crop, including cassava and maize, is located in the wetland of Jakpa, in Warri North council area of the state.

Original_Search_Title: House Maids, Blessings Or Curses?
Headline: Ghana: House Maids, Blessings Or Curses?
Body_Text:
Mini advertising boards are dotted all over city street comers, talking about agents and what they are offering the public. A message on a not-too-appealing board stuck to a tree trunk in Accra reads: 'Agent for house-helps'. I was taken aback, asking myself what exactly this so called agent was up to. Other questions darted through my mind. I tried to figure out the agent's mode of getting the 'goods', whether they go round knocking at doors asking parents if they've surplus girls between this-and-that age to be given out into labour for a handsome remuneration.
Another bother was whether complaints about the maids' misconducts will be lodged with the agent who may have been out of touch. Or parents will be made to answer for the maids' misdeeds at arbitration or before the 'I-put-it-to- your?' Not only that but also how much the agents know about these girls' character and culture to forestall unnecessary clashes. Or once it's about business such questions is much ado about nothing? What distinguishes these agents from human traffickers in other parts of the world?

Original_Search_Title: CRI Commends AGRA for Investing in Agricultural Research
Headline: Ghana: CRI Commends AGRA for Investing in Agricultural Research
Body_Text:
Kumasi — The Director at the Crop Research Institute (CRI) at the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), Dr. Hans Adu-Dappah has commended the Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa (AGRA) for investing into the research activities of Agricultural Scientist in Ghana.
According to the Director, such an initiative would enable Ghanaian researchers to develop improved varieties of seeds for a full range of Africa's important staple like beans , cassava ,cowpea, maize, rice, sweet potato and sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava - IITA Develops Cost Efficient Flash Dryer
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava - IITA Develops Cost Efficient Flash Dryer
Body_Text:
The International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) has developed cost effective and efficient flash dryer that produces 300% more cassava flour while using 40% less fuel.
Cassava farmers and processors are set to earn bigger profits and get better-quality product from this new technology developed by scientists working at the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture and national partners in Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Global Meltdown - Zambia Seeks Help
Headline: Nigeria: Global Meltdown - Zambia Seeks Help
Body_Text:
Abuja — Zambian President, Rupiah Bwezani Banda, on his first foreign trip since he came into power in October, yesterday requested for Nigeria 's assistance to tackle effect of the global economic crisis on his country's economy.
This is as President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua said despite the stalemate in Zimbabwe's peace talk over power-sharing, the progress recorded deserves commendation.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Equipment Producers Online
Headline: Cameroon: Agric Equipment Producers Online
Body_Text:
A workshop to validate their data base took place last week in Douala.
Agric equipment producers in the country have decided to establish a common web site presenting their products. The website built by French expert, Eric Bernard was the object of a restitution and validation workshop that took place last 21-22 November in Douala.

Original_Search_Title: Finding New Ways to Feed HIV-Positive People
Headline: Burkina Faso: Finding New Ways to Feed HIV-Positive People
Body_Text:
Ouagadougou — High food prices and cuts in food aid to HIV-positive people are forcing relief organisations in Burkina Faso to take another look at local foods to keep people healthy.
"We are all working with sustainability in mind, and we cannot always continue to rely on external support," said Dr Joseph Aimé Bidiga, who runs the health division at the national AIDS control council, CNLS. "We must take action to allow us to do as much as possible with our modest means."

Original_Search_Title: SON Okays Imoke's NGO Cassava Flour
Headline: Nigeria: SON Okays Imoke's NGO Cassava Flour
Body_Text:
Standards Organization of Nigeria (SON) has presented a certificate to wife of Cross River State Governor, Mrs. Obioma Imoke for the Cassava flour being produced by her POWER project.
Mrs. Imoke's NGO, Partnership Opportunity for Women Empowerment Realization (POWER) which seeks to empower the women of Cross River State for economic growth, cater for the mother and child and promote education has been vigorously involved in ensuring that added value is given to the farm produce of the women in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Urhobo Task FG on Electricity
Headline: Nigeria: Urhobo Task FG on Electricity
Body_Text:
National President of the Urhobo Development Association (UDA), Chief Thomas Essi says the Urhobo ethnic nationality in Delta State is entitled to free electricity supply to all its towns and villages because the people have sacrificed their lands and waters to oil companies and agencies for oil production, which in turn, has polluted their environment and impoverished them.
Chief Essi who stated this at the inauguration of the association, formerly Urhobo Monitoring and Development Group at Effurun, therefore, charged the Federal Government to "supply our land genuine and new transformers together with other accessories and to give our people constant and affordable power supply."

Original_Search_Title: IFAD Boost Agriculture with $200 Million
Headline: Nigeria: IFAD Boost Agriculture with $200 Million
Body_Text:
The International Fund for Agriculture Development (IFAD) has earmarked the sum of $200 million to strengthen and step up agricultural sector in Nigeria.
Of this amount over $100 million has been disbursed for on-going projects on agriculture between the Federal Government and IFAD, while $50 million would be invested in improving and processing cassava in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Why Government is Fighting Anaemia
Headline: Rwanda: Why Government is Fighting Anaemia
Body_Text:
Kigali — It will take many initiatives in order to achieve Vision 2020.
The surprising one and easiest is the support of a comprehensive food fortification programme.

Original_Search_Title: IFAD Invests N5.9 Billion to Improve Its Marketing
Headline: Nigeria: IFAD Invests N5.9 Billion to Improve Its Marketing
Body_Text:
Abuja — International Fund for Agriculture Development (IFAD) says it is investing $50 million (about N5.9 billion) to improve the market accessibility of cassava and its processing in the country.
The investment would also augment plans to diversify the huge focus on cassava and spotlight other crops that would better the lives and livelihood of small-scale rural farmers.

Original_Search_Title: IITA Develops New, Fuel Efficient Cassava Dryer
Headline: Nigeria: IITA Develops New, Fuel Efficient Cassava Dryer
Body_Text:
Scientists working at the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA), Ibadan, and national partners in Nigeria, have developed a cassava flash dryer that produces 300 per cent more and better flour using 40 per cent less fuel.
A statement by IITA on Tuesday in Abuja said the improved flash dryer is capable of processing eight tonnes of raw cassava tubers into two tonnes of high-quality flour per day compared to the less than one tonne daily production of existing models.

Original_Search_Title: Sweden to Fund More Research
Headline: Uganda: Sweden to Fund More Research
Body_Text:
Kampala — SWEDEN plans to fund more scientific research to boost food security and the management of natural resources in East Africa.
"We have supported five research projects and the Lake Victoria Research Initiative (VicRes). A new agreement will be signed soon," the ambassador, Anders Johnson, said.

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi Institutes Awards for Achievers as Varsity Gets New VC
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi Institutes Awards for Achievers as Varsity Gets New VC
Body_Text:
ABAKALIKI-Ebonyi State government has instituted awards to honour categories of individuals and institutions that exhibit best virtues and values amongst their peers.
The awards includes: the cleanest local government area in the state, best local government chairman in the state, best development centre coordinator in the state, best local government area junior/senior staff, best GM/chairman of Board and best permanent secretary.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Financing Key to Economic Growth - Agwu
Headline: Nigeria: Agric Financing Key to Economic Growth - Agwu
Body_Text:
Our agricultural banking started at the beginning of this year. The experience has been most beneficial. One thing we have found out is that microfinance banking goes to the root of the economy.
You should be able to recall that at the earlier part of this year there was a timid food crisis in the country to the extent that the presidency has to order for the importation of large quantity of food stuff like rice. This is not because we do not have enough farmers in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Closing Country's National Poverty Gap
Headline: Ghana: Closing Country's National Poverty Gap
Body_Text:
Tamale — Many of the women at the workshop of the Pagsung shea butter association are in their forties and fifties. Previously, each had collected and processed shea nuts as an individual, but earned so little she could barely get by, "We found if we came together we could make more and sell more," explains Safiya Hassan, a recent university graduate who is helping the women.
The association now includes 13 groups of shea producers, all women, in Ghana's Northern Region. Together they are able to produce more than 20 tones of shea butter per month. Much of this, in the form of high quality shea soap and creams, is supplied to a Japanese company. As a group, the women are earning an additional profit of 10 Ghana cedis (US$11) for every 100 kilogrammes, compared to what they made as individual producers. That modest extra income has already changed the lives of many of the women.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Institute to Incorporate Vitamin A in All Staple Foods
Headline: Uganda: Crop Institute to Incorporate Vitamin A in All Staple Foods
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE National Crop Resources Research Institute (NaCRRI)-Namulonge plans to embark on a project to incorporate vitamin A in all staple foods.
Dr. Robert Mwanga, a researcher at the institute, says the project aims at fighting Vitamin A deficiency in children and pregnant mothers.

Original_Search_Title: Modified Crops Yield More
Headline: Uganda: Modified Crops Yield More
Body_Text:
Kampala — FOOD prices are rising. Climate change makes it harder to produce food than it was 20 years ago in many parts of Africa.
In Uganda, for example, the average size of a maize cob has become considerably smaller. Crops that did well in particular regions, like the banana, can no longer be grown successfully without expensive soil improvement and pest control techniques.

Original_Search_Title: Restaurant Menu Go Regional
Headline: Rwanda: Restaurant Menu Go Regional
Body_Text:
Kigali — The end of the 1994 genocide saw political calm return to Rwanda. And many Rwandans journeyed from exile home. Fourteen years after they settled, there are still visible tendencies that depict their lifestyles when they were in exile.
Remera, located in the suburbs of Kigali City, is an area that harbours a considerable number of former exiles. The small restaurants in the area are faced with the task of identifying with the diverse customer preferences.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Subside, Easing Fears of Emergency
Headline: Uganda: Officials Hope Emergency Will Be Avoided as Rains Subside
Body_Text:
Kampala — Heavy rains have displaced thousands and damaged crops in eastern and northern Uganda, but officials are optimistic an emergency will be avoided as better weather is predicted.
"Some people have been affected by heavy rains with some displaced, but this does not mean that we shall see an emergency because the meteorological department has already told us that the rains are at their end," Musa Ecweru, state minister for disaster preparedness and refugees, said.

Original_Search_Title: Researchers to Increase Lifespan of Cassava Tubers
Headline: Uganda: Researchers to Increase Lifespan of Cassava Tubers
Body_Text:
Kampala — RESEARCH is on to prolong the life of cassava tubers after harvest.
Dr. Yona Baguma, a researcher at the National Crop Resources Research Institute, says they have identified genes from wild cassava that can be incorporated into the local type to delay its post-harvest deterioration.

Original_Search_Title: 'I Saw My Friend Beheaded' - LRA Abductee
Headline: Uganda: Alice - 'I Saw My Friend Being Beheaded and His Body Cut Up'
Body_Text:
Gulu — Children abducted by the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) during its decades-long insurgency in the north have to live with the trauma and psychological scars long after their rescue and return to civilian life.
One such is Alice*, abducted in 2005 when she was only 12. She told IRIN about her escape from rebel captivity in northeastern Democratic Republic of Congo:

Original_Search_Title: Why We Are Tapping the Palm Tree Potentials - Anyanwu
Headline: Nigeria: Why We Are Tapping the Palm Tree Potentials - Anyanwu
Body_Text:
Chief Longinus Anyanwu spoke on the agriculture policy of Imo State government and efforts being made to boost food production and contribute to the economy of the state.
Could you throw an insight into what the Imo State government has done so far to boost food production and alleviate poverty?

Original_Search_Title: Repayment of Loans our Greatest Challenge - Napep Boss
Headline: Nigeria: Repayment of Loans our Greatest Challenge - Napep Boss
Body_Text:
Since the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) was inaugurated in 2001 to address the challenge of poverty in Nigeria, it has been working in line with the United Nations Millennium Development Goals (MDG) desire to reduce the proportion of people living in poverty by the year 2015. NAPEP has, over the years, designed various schemes and projects to achieve its goals and objectives.
These include Youth Empowerment Scheme,Capacity Enhancement Scheme, Social Welfare Service Scheme and Rural Infrastructure Development Scheme among others. The coordinator of NAPEP in Lagos State, Chief Yakub Abiola Aromashodu recently spoke on the misconceptions people have about NAPEP's activities.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Cut Off Katakwi, Amuria Roads Again
Headline: Uganda: Floods Cut Off Katakwi, Amuria Roads Again
Body_Text:
Kampala — FLOODS have again cut off the Katakwi-Amuria road after Ajelek Bridge that connects the two districts collapsed.
A total of 6,222 households in Katakwi district have been affected.

Original_Search_Title: Second Niger Bridge Still Undone? I Approved Budget for It 14 Years Ago!,
Headline: Nigeria: Second Niger Bridge Still Undone? I Approved Budget for It 14 Years Ago!,
Body_Text:
Two-time Finance Minister, Dr Kalu Idika Kalu, who, at different times, also served as minister of national planning and minister of transport, penultimate week in Abuja, spoke to Sunday Vanguard on the global economic meltdown and sundry economic issues on the domestic front.
The BGL chairman and member, Presidential Technical Committee on Niger-Delta, also spoke on the Niger Bridge even as he gave insight into what Nigerians should expect from his committee.

Original_Search_Title: Input Trade Fairs Falling Short
Headline: Eswatini: Input Trade Fairs Falling Short
Body_Text:
Mbabane — Mary Ntshangase sits under a big umbrella -- a packet of beans in one hand and a packet of peanuts in the other -- wooing customers to her stall.
She is one of the vendors displaying their wares at the input trade fair at Matsanjeni, in the drought-stricken Lubombo region of eastern Swaziland.

Original_Search_Title: Volta Basin Problems, Whose Headache? (1)
Headline: Ghana: Volta Basin Problems, Whose Headache? (1)
Body_Text:
Each group of people has a traditional vocation which distinguishes them from others. If you are a costal dweller you may be a fisherman or a hunter if your geographical location is by a forest. This explains reasons for which people resist attempts by governments to relocate them from their ancestral locations no matter how well the intentions. The case of the Kabakas of Uganda needs no elaboration.
If you were a bit old and ever traveled across the Sogakope Bridge in 1970s you would have noticed the commonness of oysters, known as 'adordi' or 'aforli', sold in various forms. Indeed, it was a huge business among the people, particularly the women folks.

Original_Search_Title: Over 1.5 Million in Benefit From Return of Cassava, UN Reports
Headline: Africa: Over 1.5 Million in Benefit From Return of Cassava, UN Reports
Body_Text:
Following years of massive crop losses caused by a devastating virus, the tropical root crop cassava - one of Africa's principal foodstuffs - has made a comeback and is benefiting some 1.65 million people throughout the Great Lakes region, the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) reported today.
"Having cassava back on the table is of major importance, especially to the region's most vulnerable, who have been hit hard by this year's global food crisis," said Eric Kueneman, Chief of the FAO Crop and Grassland Service.

Original_Search_Title: Disease Devastates Cassava Crop, Threatens Widespread Hunger
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Disease Devastates Cassava Crop, Threatens Widespread Hunger
Body_Text:
Brazzaville — Cassava is the staple food for most people in the Republic of Congo, but this main source of nourishment is being threatened by a disease that has spread to most areas of the country.
"We've been having problems for more than two years and it's getting worse with the mosaic destroying our crops," said Rose Ambeto, who has several cassava fields.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Strike Again, Kill Two
Headline: Uganda: Floods Strike Again, Kill Two
Body_Text:
Kampala — Floods that ravaged the north and northeastern region last year are back, killing two UPDF soldiers in Nakapiripirit and Bukwo districts.
Three districts of Kotido, Abim and Kaabong are now inaccessible from Moroto.

Original_Search_Title: Corridors of Power
Headline: Uganda: Corridors of Power
Body_Text:
Kampala — Animal farm
Local government minister Kahinda Otafiire on Tuesday amused Parliament when he said Kampala City Council (KCC) had turned the city into an 'animal farm'. "Tourists are welcomed by flower gardens fenced with barbed wire instead of green gardens. The impression one gets in a city is an enclosure of animals. "In Europe and other cities green natural vegetation is treasured. In the United Arab Emirates green is treasure number one, while in Uganda the natural beauty scenery is replaced by barbed wire. Otafiire was requesting parliament to approve $ 33.6m to revamp the city.

Original_Search_Title: Dutch Youth Get the Feel of Ugandan Life
Headline: Uganda: Dutch Youth Get the Feel of Ugandan Life
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDA is a beautiful country, says Jolanda Vian Schie, a citizen of the Netherlands. And to show how much she appreciates the country Jolanda who has been in and out of Uganda for the last six years married a Ugandan.
So strong is Jolanda's attraction that she brought youth from Netherlands to see Uganda's beauty. Through the Netherlands Young Men's Christian Association (YMCA), Jolanda initiated a partnership with YMCA Uganda. The associations started a Ugandan-Dutch exchange programme for young people to visit and learn about each other's way of life.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Halt Moyo Road Works
Headline: Uganda: Floods Halt Moyo Road Works
Body_Text:
Kampala — FLOODS have stopped the construction of the Moyo-Obongi road.
The 77-kilometre project, that is to cost sh500m, stalled in September when the River Nile burst its banks due to heavy rains and displaced people in Itula and Gimara sub-counties in Obongi.

Original_Search_Title: Locals Sue Shell in Dutch Court Over Oil Spills
Headline: Nigeria: Locals Sue Shell in Dutch Court Over Oil Spills
Body_Text:
AN environmentalist group and four Nigerians filed suit against Royal Dutch Shell PLC in The Netherlands on Friday, claiming the company was negligent in cleaning up oil spills in Nigeria.
The civil suit filed by the four men and Friends of the Earth is unusual in that it seeks to hold Shell's parent company liable for damages allegedly caused by its Nigerian subsidiary.

Original_Search_Title: Engineer Wants Citizens to Emphasise Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Engineer Wants Citizens to Emphasise Agriculture
Body_Text:
Mr Anthony Shobo, the country's first chemical engineer, has called on Nigerians to be more involved in agriculture to ensure greater economic balance.
Shobo, who graduated as a chemical engineer in 1957, told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN), at Effurun in Delta State, that the country should shift emphasis from crude oil since agriculture was more profitable.

Original_Search_Title: Country Needs a Blend of Sources of Power, Says Bakare, MD, Nigerian Wire and Cable
Headline: Nigeria: Country Needs a Blend of Sources of Power, Says Bakare, MD, Nigerian Wire and Cable
Body_Text:
Meeting him for the first time, you might mistake him for an undergraduate, because he does not look his age.
Born in Ilesha in Osun State, Lateef Akande Bakare, a chartered accountant, is 53 years old. He is a director in many blue chips companies in the country which cut across the various sectors of the economy, but currently managing director/CEO of the Nigerian Wire & Cable, which he was appointed to turn around last year.

Original_Search_Title: Export Path Trains Nigerians for Agoa Benefits
Headline: Nigeria: Export Path Trains Nigerians for Agoa Benefits
Body_Text:
Export Path, a consortiums of several organisations Vertical Optimisation, Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC), the Nigerian Embassy in Washington DC, US Department of Commerce, NEXIM Bank, and Diamond Bank, has began the process of training Nigerian exporters to enable them access to the United States market and reap some benefits under the African Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA).
The agency provides exporters with three things, viz: It helps them develop products that can sell in the US. "We provide them financing to manufacture the product and help them sell it. That is what Export Path does," according to Emeka Nwankwo, who is coordinating the training.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Prepares to Approve Biosafety Legislation
Headline: Kenya: Govt Prepares to Approve Biosafety Legislation
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Kenyan government plans to pass its proposed biosafety bill next month (December) following years of delays.
Mary Kamau, a director from the research and technical training division of the Kenyan Ministry of Agriculture, said the Biosafety Bill 2008 should be passed before the Kenyan parliament breaks up for Christmas. After that it will go to President Mwai Kibaki for approval or rejection.

Original_Search_Title: We Could Go the Amin, Obote Way
Headline: Uganda: We Could Go the Amin, Obote Way
Body_Text:
For three whole days, Radio Uganda blared the startling news by Idi Amin's military spokesman, normally a euphemism for the Big Daddy himself, that a fleet of American, Chinese, Indian and Israeli planes had been sighted over the Indian Ocean near Mombasa, heading to attack Uganda.
It became the slowest flight of military planes in history because to date none of the alleged planes has arrived anywhere in Uganda to discharge their deadly payload. This was one of the favourite antics that delighted Amin to dramatize himself at a time when his blood drenched regime was besieged with innumerable difficulties just before it collapsed from its misadventure with Tanzania.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens Urged to Vote for PNC
Headline: Ghana: Citizens Urged to Vote for PNC
Body_Text:
Hajia Alima Ali, National Women Organiser of the People's National Convention (PNC), has called on Ghanaians to vote for the party on 7 December for peace and unity.
She also urged the electorate to vote against the New Patriotic Party (NPP) and National Democratic Congress (NDC) in the December 7 polls because the two parties were not trustworthy.

Original_Search_Title: Soroti Scientists to Revive Cotton Growing
Headline: Uganda: Soroti Scientists to Revive Cotton Growing
Body_Text:
Scientists in Soroti are set to start a research in the genetically modified cotton in a bid to rescue farmers from the conventional cotton which had suffered a set back in production due to the ball warm pest thereby resulting into poor yields.
The Director of the National Semi Arid Resources Research Institute, Serere (NaSARRI), Dr Tom Areke with his team have already ploughed approximately one acre of land on the institute land for the field trials.

Original_Search_Title: Closing the National Poverty Gap
Headline: Ghana: Closing the National Poverty Gap
Body_Text:
Tamale — Many of the women at the workshop of the Pagsung shea butter association are in their forties and fifties. Previously, each had collected and processed shea nuts as an individual, but earned so little she could barely get by. "We found if we came together we could make more and sell more," explains Safiya Hassan, a recent university graduate who is helping the women.
Mixing shea butter for soap production in Tamale, the capital of Ghana's Northern Region: For poor women, even modest increases in their incomes can make a big difference.

Original_Search_Title: A United Front Gives a Better Platform
Headline: Uganda: A United Front Gives a Better Platform
Body_Text:
Can Ugandan farmers unite and form a strong body and can they influence the government to come up with farmer friendly policies? Can farmers make an impact on government decisions about such issues as the environment, biotechnology or budgetary allocations to agriculture?
Are they capable of lobbying for resources to improve production and productivity? Are they in position to create strong marketing systems for their produce within and outside the country? Are they empowered to determine their own prices for their produce?

Original_Search_Title: Rebels Beheaded Civilians, Says Victim
Headline: Sierra Leone: Rebels Beheaded Civilians, Says Victim
Body_Text:
The Hague — A war victim has told the trial for former Liberian President Charles Taylor in The Hague that rebels decapitated human beings during the war in Sierra Leone.
The prosecution witness Mustapha Mansaray, 60, said in 1993 rebels hacked off the heads of civilians including his uncle, Jibril Turay and connected them to a rope used to man checkpoints in Maola, Pujehun district.

Original_Search_Title: Country Produces More Rice
Headline: Uganda: Country Produces More Rice
Body_Text:
Kampala — RICE production in Uganda has increased from 50,000 to 170,000 tonnes per annum.
This has made the country reduce rice imports from 60,000 metric tones in 2005 to 35,000 tonnes in 2007, saving about $30m in the process and improving food security, the state minister for fisheries, Fred Mukisa, has said.

Original_Search_Title: Kyabazinga Storm Brews
Headline: Uganda: Kyabazinga Storm Brews
Body_Text:
They depend on fene (jack fruit) for survival. Sometimes jiggers harass them. The main tarmac road passing through the sub-region is used for drying cassava because there is little economic activity.
Poverty is the main story. But they have regularly overwhelmingly voted NRM and for President Yoweri Museveni. These fellows are known as the Basoga peasants. However, it appears their marriage is getting rocky.

Original_Search_Title: Abacha Years - How Wole Soyinka Relocated to US
Headline: Nigeria: Abacha Years - How Wole Soyinka Relocated to US
Body_Text:
Professor Wole Soyinka - the Nobel Prize winner - flew into Frankfurt, Germany on October 15 to receive the first Maikgrafin Wilhelmine prize awarded him by the city and University of Bayreuth.
As he came out of the plane, Soyinka walked briskly pass the arrival hall. He was the first person to pass through the entry gate. He was received by Mr. Gerd Meuer, his journalist friend and German translator of over four decades. For Meuer, that was not a surprise because he had known Soyinka to be a "light traveller" over the years. He knew Soyinka as someone who travels "with a tiny cabin-troller, so he doesn't have to wait for any luggage."

Original_Search_Title: Investigate Teacher for Using Pupils for Labour- Parent
Headline: Ghana: Investigate Teacher for Using Pupils for Labour- Parent
Body_Text:
Mrs Sarah Abban, a parent, has appealed to the Director-General of the Ghana Education Service (GES) to launch a full scale investigations into the alleged use of pupils for labour "on contract hours" by the head teacher of the Gomoa Brofoyeduru Catholic Primary School.
She said using pupils during school hours was against the rules and regulations of GES, but Mr John K Fenu, head teacher is alleged of doing so.

Original_Search_Title: Climate Change Can Be Managed
Headline: Uganda: Climate Change Can Be Managed
Body_Text:
Kampala — UGANDANS have been urged to take advantage of the current rise in global food prices to grow food which will supplement their salary as well as other income.
But Africa, and indeed the world, still faces a stiff challenge of sustaining food production in the light of climate change.

Original_Search_Title: Lease Holders Destroying Forests
Headline: Uganda: Lease Holders Destroying Forests
Body_Text:
Hoima — Patrick Katabaazi,32, has a rice garden in Kasogoire Forest Reserve in Hoima District. "The soils here are very fertile, I expect to harvest more than 200 bags out of this (rice) garden" Mr Katabaazi says. He anticipates to earn about Shs2 million if he sales his harvest at 1,000 per kilogram. Little does he mind that his garden is in an environment conservation area.
He is among several people who the National Forestry Authority (NFA) officials are accusing of encroachment and failing the conservation efforts. This is after the NFA offered land in selected central forest reserves to private developers to plant trees for sustainable conservation efforts.

Original_Search_Title: Army Promotes Agriculture in Karamoja Region
Headline: Uganda: Army Promotes Agriculture in Karamoja Region
Body_Text:
Karamoja — In an unprecedented move, the Uganda People's Defence Forces has joined hands with residents in the famine stricken Karamoja region to engage in mass agriculture.
Despite the long spells of drought in the sub-region, this year's harvests from a few farmers visited during the tour of the area reflect that the region has fertile soils that can support the growth of sim sim, beans, maize, sunflower, millet, sorghum, potatoes, onions and cassava among others.

Original_Search_Title: Nyarugenge Residents Query KCC's Valuation Process
Headline: Rwanda: Nyarugenge Residents Query KCC's Valuation Process
Body_Text:
Kigali — Residents of Nyarugenge District who are supposed to be relocated to pave way for for the implementation of the City's 50-year master plan have complained that the valuation of their property is being done unfairly.
The residents from the ten sectors of Nyarugenge district told the Mayor Nyarugenge, Origene Rutayisire, and the Vice Mayor of Kigali City Council Jeanne d'Arc Gakuba, that the valuation specialists are valuing property without the owners' participation.

Original_Search_Title: Villagers Resort to Wild Fruits As Food Shortages Worsen
Headline: Zimbabwe: Villagers Resort to Wild Fruits As Food Shortages Worsen
Body_Text:
Dubula Dumezweni Sibanda (77) holds his head in his hands as he narrates the ordeal people in Lupane District and millions of other Zimbabweans are faced with as a result of hunger due to acute food shortages.
Sibanda, who is taking care of six grandchildren, says people in his district are now surviving on wild fruits and roots from edible local plants and trees.

Original_Search_Title: Will Jatropha Invade?
Headline: Mozambique: Will Jatropha Invade?
Body_Text:
Reflecting on the fifth International Via Campesina Conference held in Maputo, Mozambique, on 19 October, John E. Peck examines the ongoing struggle around 'biofuels' and unpacks the extent to which global discourses on solutions climate change have been commandeered by multinational corporations. With a particular focus on Jatropha, the author explores the crop's likely impact in Mozambique and the consequences for food sovereignty, land use, and contested resources, and argues that true progress towards sustainable agriculture will only be attained once governments cease to emphasise agro-fuel subsidies.
On 19 October 2008, at the opening ceremony of the Fifth International Via Campesina Conference in Maputo, Mozambique, over 600 representatives from 50-plus countries were gathered to hear a welcome address by the President of the Republic of Mozambique, Armando EmÃƒÂ­io Guebuza. While President Guebuza had some encouraging remarks about the future potential of peasant agriculture, his suggestion that Jatropha was a solution for Mozambique's energy crisis was not well received by many in the audience. Jatropha is but one of a whole host of crops (including maize, soya, canola, sugarcane, cassava, sunflower, palm, coconut, and castor among others) now being aggressively promoted as feedstock for the global agro-fuel industrial complex. Such crops, often genetically engineered, grown in monoculture plantations, and destined for export markets, hardly deserve to be called 'biofuels' since they have no life-affirming qualities and undermine all the basic principles of food sovereignty.

Original_Search_Title: Clandestine Cannabis Farmers Feed Growing Drug Abuse
Headline: Africa: Clandestine Cannabis Farmers Feed Growing Drug Abuse
Body_Text:
Praia — Farmers in West Africa are turning to cannabis as a quick cash crop, feeding the biggest illegal drug market in the world.
UN Office on Drugs and Crime's (UNODC) director for West Africa, Antonio Mazzitelli, told IRIN clandestine farmers are lured by quick earnings: "Faced with the choice of cannabis or cassava, some choose easy money."

Original_Search_Title: NEPC Tasks Tobacco Manufacturers on Diversification
Headline: Nigeria: NEPC Tasks Tobacco Manufacturers on Diversification
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Export Pro-motion Council (NEPC) has urged tobacco manufacturers in the country to diversify into other non-oil export businesses that can generate jobs, good profit and foreign exchange earnings.
They also said NEPC can initiate establishment of Industrial Parks in a Public/Private Partnership arrangement to help the small and medium enterprises ameliorate their challenges given the current state of infrastructure facilities. This could be taken as part of the tobacco sector's social responsibility to the Nigerian community.

Original_Search_Title: Survival Recipe Book
Headline: Zimbabwe: Survival Recipe Book
Body_Text:
Bulawayo — Rural Zimbabweans have always turned to an emergency larder of wild foods to see them through hard times, but in this year of shortages and dizzying prices for all basic foodstuffs, the fruits and roots foraged from the bush are keeping many alive.
In the southern province of Matabeleland North, villagers are relying on a variety of wild fruits, tubers and okra-like vegetables, which become more abundant as the rainy season progresses.

Original_Search_Title: Students Commend Varsity's Work-Study Programme
Headline: Nigeria: Students Commend Varsity's Work-Study Programme
Body_Text:
Lagos — Three years after the university established the Obafemi Awolowo University Students' Support Services (OAUSSS), also known as the Work-Study Programme, some beneficiaries testify that the initiative not only provides financial assistance, but life-long skills as well. Uchechukwu Nnaike reports
The Obafemi Awolowo University Students' Support Services (OAUSSS), also known as the Work-Study Programme, was conceived in 2000 when the former Vice-Chancellor, Prof. Roger Makanjuola invited Dr. Stella Williams to prepare a proposal. It eventually began during the 2004/2005 session, when a committee was set up to start it with 300 indigent students.

Original_Search_Title: 'Agric, Food Security Remains Nepad's Priorities'
Headline: Africa: 'Agric, Food Security Remains Nepad's Priorities'
Body_Text:
Jos — National Coordinator of New Partnership for African Development (NEPAD) and Special Assistant to President Umaru Yar'Adua on NEPAD, Ambassador Tunji Olagbunji, has described agriculture and food security as top priority issues of the programme in Africa.
Olagbunji stated this at a two day retreat for NEPAD-Nigeria senior officers held at the National Institute for Policy and Strategies Studies( NIPSS) Kuru in Plateau State.

Original_Search_Title: Ijwere Pleads for Development of Niger Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Ijwere Pleads for Development of Niger Delta
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ten years after the October 17, 1998 pipeline fire disaster that occurred at Atiwor in Jesse, a satellite village in Idjerhe clan in Delta State, leaders from across the country gathered recently at the spot to mark the 10th memorial service.
The essence of the Jesse Summit organised by Green News in collaboration with the New Partnership for Africa's Development (NEPAD) was to fine-tune the best possible ways of creating new investment opportunities that would promote sustainable development for the community.

Original_Search_Title: Benue Agric Varsity VC Canvasses Self Sufficiency in Food Production
Headline: Nigeria: Benue Agric Varsity VC Canvasses Self Sufficiency in Food Production
Body_Text:
Makurdi — The Vice-Chancellor of the University of Agriculture, Makurdi (UAM), Professor Daniel Uza has called on Nigerians to learn from the lessons of its past failures and plan very well to achieve self sufficiency in food production by the year 2020.
He said this in a paper titled, "Food Security Policies and Programmes in Nigeria", delivered at an International Conference on Food Security at the McGill University in Montreal, Canada.

Original_Search_Title: Guebuza Launches 2008/09 Agricultural Campaign
Headline: Mozambique: Guebuza Launches 2008/09 Agricultural Campaign
Body_Text:
Nicoadala — Hunger is one of the most cruel expressions of poverty, and one of the greatest obstacles to freeing energy and initiative for the country's social and economic development, Mozambican President Armando Guebuza declared on Friday
Speaking at the official launch of the 2008/2009 agricultural campaign in Nicoadala, in the central province of Zambezia, Guebuza stressed the continuing reality of hunger in Mozambique, with many families, in both rural and urban areas, unsure where their next meal will come from.

Original_Search_Title: Lagos Still Committed to Feeding the Nation -Fashola's Wife
Headline: Nigeria: Lagos Still Committed to Feeding the Nation -Fashola's Wife
Body_Text:
Tuesday 21st of October would ever remain fresh in the memory of Itedo-Alaafia/Dado community in Badagry in Lagos State when the first Modern community based agro-processing center was commissioned as part of practical demonstration of the Lagos State government to empower farmers and agro-processors that are predominantly women and are rural-based in Lagos State.
First lady of Lagos, Mrs Abimbola Fashola while commissioning the Cassava processing center said it is part of the efforts of the administration of his excellency, Mr Babatunde Raji Fashola SAN to improve the quality of lives of the people particularly women farmers who engage in the processing of Cassava to produce more with less effort and also make more money from their labour.

Original_Search_Title: Local Art Mirrors the Emotions of Independence
Headline: Uganda: Local Art Mirrors the Emotions of Independence
Body_Text:
As Uganda celebrated her 46th Independence anniversary slightly over two weeks ago, the country's contemporary artists convened at Nommo Gallery and exhibited works depicting the emotions that followed the attainment of self-rule.
On display was an intriguing repertoire of abstract and semi-abstract paintings, metal and wooden sculptures that evoked the deep sorrows of what the country endured on her road to independence, and of course, the revelry that accompanied this achievement. The words of one critic that authentic art must ignite an argument between an artist and his audience struck the mind by just looking at Yusuf Ssali's painting.

Original_Search_Title: Displaced First By War, Now By Elephants
Headline: Uganda: Displaced First By War, Now By Elephants
Body_Text:
Amuru — Marauding elephants in northern Uganda have added to the challenges faced by civilians trying to rebuild their lives in the wake of 20 years of civil war, destroying their crops and prompting some to return to displaced people's (IDP) camps they had only recently left.
"The villagers are scared of the elephants; some of them have sought refuge in huts they had left in the [IDP] camps," John Bosco Okullo, a local leader in Amuru District told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: What to Do With the Land Use Act?
Headline: Nigeria: What to Do With the Land Use Act?
Body_Text:
On 29th March 1978 a rather monstrous and bizarre law of general application over Land in Nigeria, entitled The Land Use Act. 1978 came into effect.
Its explanatory Note goes thus: "The Act vests all land comprised in the Territory of each State (except land vested in the Federal Government or its Agencies) solely in the Governor of the State who would hold such land IN TRUST (Capitals of Supplied) for the people. The Governor would henceforth be responsible for allocation of land in all urban areas to individuals resident in the State or to Organizations for residential, agricultural, commercial and other purposes while similar powers with respect to non-urban areas are conferred on Local Government." This explanation was largely enacted in the opening clauses (Section 1 thereof) of the Act.

Original_Search_Title: Saraki Gets Food Science Institute Award, Pledges More for Agric
Headline: Nigeria: Saraki Gets Food Science Institute Award, Pledges More for Agric
Body_Text:
Kwara State Governor, Dr. Bukola Saraki, at Ogbomoso, received the Presidential Award of the Nigerian Institute of Food Science and Technology (NIFST) for Excellence in Agriculture and Food Security Initiatives at its 32nd Conference/AGM, holding in Ladoke Akintola University of Technology (LAUTECH), which began on Tuesday and ended on Thursday, 16th October, 2008.
Food scientists and technologists drawn from universities and polytechnics in the country, scholars, food manufacturers, ministries of agriculture, agencies, research organisations and students are attending the three-day conference, declared open by Osun State Deputy Governor, Erelu Olusola Obada, representing Governor Olagunsoye Oyinlola.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Opens 700 Million Plant Lab in Buloba
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Opens 700 Million Plant Lab in Buloba
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has opened a laboratory that produces plant tissue.
The technology involves the production of multiple plants from a single plant in the absence of seeds or pollinators to produce seeds.

Original_Search_Title: Keeping It Organic
Headline: Uganda: Keeping It Organic
Body_Text:
Farmers working to boost food security and improve their livelihoods in terms of incomes from the produce will now move their minds from the use of chemicals to natural organisms.
Research carried out indicates that in order to maintain food security in the country and the world over, farmers have been boosted by biological control measures of pests and diseases. Mr Kamayombi Bulegeya, a crop protection specialist says farmers can now focus on environmentally friendly methods other than the use of chemicals.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Ravage Southwestern Districts
Headline: Uganda: Rains Ravage Southwestern Districts
Body_Text:
Isingiro — The torrential rains that have hit the southwestern districts have left thousands of locals staring hunger in the face following the destruction of their crops. The rains, which started in September, are also likely to force many of the residents to seek alternative accommodation since many homes have been destroyed.
In Isingiro and Mbarara districts, over 3,000 homes lost their gardens of beans, cassava, Irish potatoes and bananas in a series of stormy rains that hit Kabuyanda and Mwizi sub-counties.

Original_Search_Title: Scientists Prepare to Fight Cotton Disease and Pests
Headline: Uganda: Scientists Prepare to Fight Cotton Disease and Pests
Body_Text:
Scientists in Uganda will soon begin testing the genetically modified cotton in their laboratories to prove whether they are resistant to cotton wilt and other related cotton diseases, which has been destroying the conventional cotton plant.
The government recently gave a go ahead for scientists to carry out the above research just as they have been doing to other crops such as the East African Highland banana and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: UN Helps in Local Effort to Rid Country of Marijuana
Headline: Liberia: UN Helps in Local Effort to Rid Country of Marijuana
Body_Text:
The United Nations is assisting Liberian National Police (LNP) tackle the drug trade in remote areas of the West African nation, with the latest joint raid bringing the total amount of marijuana seized to almost 1,000 kilograms.
UN Police (UNPOL) and a Bangladeshi battalion and a Nigerian formed police unit (FPU) serving with the UN Mission in Liberia (UNMIL) took part in the LNP-coordinated surprise operation, one of several recent efforts seeking to identify and destroy marijuana plantations, UNPOL reported today.

Original_Search_Title: Councils Tasked On Food Sufficiency
Headline: Nigeria: Councils Tasked On Food Sufficiency
Body_Text:
Lagos — Local government chieftains in Akwa Ibom State have been advised to identify and intensify efforts in the cultivation of food crops with comparative advantage that can thrive well in their localities.
Governor Godswill Akpabio stated this, while declaring open a one-day state dialogue on poverty reduction and food security to mark the 2008 World Poverty Day at the Idongesit Nkanga Secretariat, Uyo.

Original_Search_Title: State Releases Fertilizer for Sale
Headline: Kenya: State Releases Fertilizer for Sale
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The government has released fertilizer worth Sh396 million to be sold to farmers in Central, Eastern and South Rift regions at a subsidised price.
Speaking in Meru over the weekend when he received 5,400 tonnes of the fertilizer for the upper eastern region, Agriculture Assistant minister Japheth Kareke said the consignment will only be sold to genuine farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Isingiro Residents Get Relief Food
Headline: Uganda: Isingiro Residents Get Relief Food
Body_Text:
Kampala — OVER 3,000 residents from Isingiro south constituency have received food aid following a hailstorm that killed two people and destroyed most food crops.
The area MP, Alex Byarugaba, handed over the items to the beneficiaries on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Sector in Crisis
Headline: Liberia: Agriculture Sector in Crisis
Body_Text:
As the Liberian government is deeply striving to piece together strategies through the Poverty Reduction Strategy (PRS) of how to reduce the high rate of poverty amongst its citizens, Liberia's Agriculture Minister Chris Toe has highlights challenges facing the agriculture sector of the state.
Dr, Chris Toe said that the farming activities in the country is under serious challenge adding that the 15 years of bitter civil conflict has also greatly help to increase the difficulties for farmers in the West African "Liberia".

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Visits Rukungiri Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Museveni Visits Rukungiri Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni, who is currently in Rukungiri District to sensitise people on the Prosperity for All programme, yesterday visited the 120-acre farm of Canon David Kawaawa of Nyaruzinga Parish, Ruhinda Sub-county.
Kawaawa earns sh65m per year from fish farming, cattle, goat and sheep rearing activities as well as banana cultivation.

Original_Search_Title: WFP to Double Food Purchases in Country
Headline: Uganda: WFP to Double Food Purchases in Country
Body_Text:
Kampala — The United Nations World Food Programme (WFP) has pledged to double the amount of food it buys in Uganda to about $100 million (Shs170 billion) worth annually.
WFP is already the single largest buyer of food in Uganda. Last year, the agency bought food worth nearly $55 million (Shs93.5 billion, in 2006, purchases totalled nearly $48 million (Shs81.6 billion).

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices High Amid 'Cheap' Fuel
Headline: Uganda: Food Prices High Amid 'Cheap' Fuel
Body_Text:
Kampala — DESPITE a fall in fuel prices by about sh300, commodity and transport costs have remained high, a weekly mini-survey has shown.
The price of oil on the international market has declined to around $68 per barrel from over $120 previously due to the global financial crisis.

Original_Search_Title: Women Acquire New Techniques on Food Processing
Headline: Cameroon: Women Acquire New Techniques on Food Processing
Body_Text:
The training was organised in Yaounde yesterday to mark this year's International Day of Rural Women.
Rural women, most if not all of them from women associations in the Centre province gathered at the Yaounde Chamber of Agriculture yesterday to receive lessons on food processing and conservation. Organised by the Chamber of Agriculture, Livestock and Forest, the exercise sets out to build the capacity of rural women with special focus on processing, storage and conservation of some agricultural products. Pride of place went to cocoa, cassava and vegetables.

Original_Search_Title: 'I Smell a Fantastic Aroma'
Headline: Africa: 'I Smell a Fantastic Aroma'
Body_Text:
Mombasa — As Africa and the rest of the world seek to end the current food crisis -- which could get worse with the flu sweeping across global financial markets -- it is time Africa made gains from the food crisis.
Peter Hartmann, Director General of the influential global research group, International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) spoke with IPS reporter Busani Bafana on the sidelines of the Pan-African Banana Conference on partnerships to increase research impact, held in Mombasa, Kenya from Oct. 5-10.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Shifts From Aid to Food Production
Headline: Uganda: WFP Shifts From Aid to Food Production
Body_Text:
Kampala — Shirtless Karimojong men pant and pace at the World Food Programme (WFP) warehouses. They are on a frantic mission to load a truck with maize, split peas, corn, soya blend and cooking oil donations from western countries.
It does not take long to fill the truck with an estimated 30 metric tons of cargo. Soon, as it pulls away from the sun-beaten warehouse, the men pull up the massive back shutter and knock in the metal bolts. They then quickly gather and tighten the tarpaulin over the load with ropes to ensure the food is safely tucked in.

Original_Search_Title: WFP to Buy More Local Food
Headline: Uganda: WFP to Buy More Local Food
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE UN's World Food Programme (WFP) wants to double the amount of food it buys in Uganda, to about $100m (sh170b) worth annually, to support agriculture in the country and improving the lives of small-scale farmers.
This was announced yesterday by the country director, Stanlake Samkange, ahead of World Food Day, marked today.

Original_Search_Title: Kibirige Was a Patriot, Says Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Kibirige Was a Patriot, Says Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni yesterday scoffed at unpatriotic politicians who only stay in Uganda during "fair-weather" times.
Addressing mourners at the burial of former agriculture minister Kibirige Ssebunya in Katente village, Mukono district, Museveni hailed him as a patriot who stayed in Uganda during turbulent times and continued to carry out research.

Original_Search_Title: New Bean Varieties Boost Household Income for Farmers
Headline: Uganda: New Bean Varieties Boost Household Income for Farmers
Body_Text:
Farmers are generating more income from beans after the introduction of new bean varieties, according to research by the National Agricultural Research Organisation (Naro) and other partners such as the International Centre for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT).
"New varieties account for 67 per cent of annual household bean income; and 45 per cent of household bean consumption," according to the research findings published in Highlights CIAT in Africa. "Poor households earned Shs40,000 ($23) from beans annually, compared to Shs60,000-70,000 per year ($34 - $40) earned by wealthy households, indicating that beans contribute more to poverty reduction given that the rich have bigger bean plots and other sources of income."

Original_Search_Title: 90 Percent of Raw Materials Used in Country Imported, Says RMRDC
Headline: Nigeria: 90 Percent of Raw Materials Used in Country Imported, Says RMRDC
Body_Text:
The Director-General of Raw Material Research and Development Council (RMRDC), Professor Peter Owualu has decried the increase in the importation of raw materials used by engineering companies in Nigeria, saying that over 90% of them are imported.
The Director-General, who also noted that roadside fabricators of industrial material are threats to the sector, said that unless Nigeria has a proper plant design, it would never address the problem.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Growth is More Than Cash Flow
Headline: Uganda: Economic Growth is More Than Cash Flow
Body_Text:
Kampala — I read with interest Dr. Augustus Nuwagaba's opinion titled "Why Economic Growth Is Not Felt In People's Pockets" which was published in the New Vision of September 15. Nuwagaba gave an inaccurate analysis.
First, he wrongly characterises economic growth as construction of roads, buildings, airports, and other public facilities! What he characterises as economic growth is physical infrastructure development, which is only an indirect productive capital that facilitates the production of goods and services in a country.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Leave Families Homeless
Headline: Burundi: Heavy Rains Leave Families Homeless and in Need of Aid
Body_Text:
Bujumbura — Hundreds of families are without assistance in the northwestern province of Cibitoke after heavy rains damaged houses and crops, officials said.
The administrator of the Mabayi commune in Cibitoke, Jean-Marie Vianney Hategekimana, said exact figures were not available, adding: "We are still assessing the damage and the needs of the population."

Original_Search_Title: Jesse Summit Will Boost Economy, Says Omonhinmin
Headline: Nigeria: Jesse Summit Will Boost Economy, Says Omonhinmin
Body_Text:
For Niger Delta region's continuous socio-economic development to go on sustainably, we as humans must provide a framework for harmonising the basic web's-environmental needs and the human satisfactions with the intent of preserving and enriching the productive capacity of the both from generation to generation.
Aiyegbeni Omonhinmin, Programme Co-ordinator, Jesse Sustainable Green Summit 2008 in this interview with Senior Correspondent, Sylvester Enoghase, insists that the summit will create jobs, boost investment, develop modern micro-business capacity and promote a sustainable, enlightened attitude that would change the behaviour of our local communities as well as some of their habits and age-long cultural traditions which contribute to the continuous degradation of the environment. Excerpts:

Original_Search_Title: Nsibambi Hails Kibirige
Headline: Uganda: Nsibambi Hails Kibirige
Body_Text:
Kampala — Prime Minister Apolo Nsibambi has commended former agriculture minister Kibirige Ssebunya for his exceptional research and development initiatives.
Kibirige succumbed to cancer last Wednesday at Mulago Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: Experts Want Law on Modified Foods
Headline: Uganda: Experts Want Law on Modified Foods
Body_Text:
Kampala — EXPERTS have called for a law to regulate genetically modified organisms.
Arthur Makara, the executive director of Science for Development, said the law would address bio-safety concerns. "The law would help in case there are violations and address any risks," he told participants at a workshop held at Shanghai Restaurant in Kampala last week.

Original_Search_Title: Checkmating the Impending Cataclysm
Headline: Nigeria: Checkmating the Impending Cataclysm
Body_Text:
One of the biggest obstacles to our national development is that our leaders don't tell the people they govern the truth.
Often, these leaders have opted to keep the people in the dark over what is happening in the polity. The situation becomes more worrisome when the issue involved is of topical national importance. Curiously, they betray no emotions, whatsoever, about their perfidious actions. Rather they carry on as if there is nothing is at stake.

Original_Search_Title: When Neo-Liberal Political Parties Crave for Choppers to Impress!
Headline: Tanzania: When Neo-Liberal Political Parties Crave for Choppers to Impress!
Body_Text:
Today, let us address the violence and turmoil that has dominated headlines in the electronic and print media in the intervening period in the heat of campaigns for the by-election in Tarime, northern Tanzania, which took place yesterday.
In doing so, let us address the colouring of the political parties, whether they have any ideology at all or anything of substance.

Original_Search_Title: The North And Present Challenges
Headline: Nigeria: The North And Present Challenges
Body_Text:
