The foundation for Northern development was laid by the Sadauna of Sokoto, Sir Ahmadu Bello. He put in place Ahmadu Bello University, New Nigeria Newspapers, Northern Nigerian Development Corporation, School of Agric Zaria with a campus in Kabba Province etc.
While all this was going on, he allowed everybody to be himself and adhere to his or her own faith. That was why we had the likes of Daniel Ogbadu, Peter Achimugu, Michael Buba and Sunday Awoniyi co-habiting, planning and visioning with their Muslim brothers and sisters in the North without friction. All this is now history as hawks among Northern military and civilian politicians have introduced one marginalisation policy or the other to divide and rule the giant North. This has done monumental damage to our unity of purpose.

Original_Search_Title: What the NSSF Probe Found
Headline: Uganda: What the NSSF Probe Found
Body_Text:
Below is an abridged version of the draft report of the investigations into the Temangalo land saga
BACKGROUND

Original_Search_Title: World Bank Awards $4 Million to Agricultural Projects
Headline: Africa: World Bank Awards $4 Million to Agricultural Projects
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The World Bank has awarded $4m to innovative agricultural projects across the globe, including three based in Uganda, Tanzania and Nigeria.
The Bank, under its Development Marketplace Programme, a competitive grant scheme aimed at financing innovation in development activities, awarded the money to 22 innovative projects from 16 countries, with the majority originating from Latin America and the Caribbean.

Original_Search_Title: Bagisu, Bagwere Clash
Headline: Uganda: Bagisu, Bagwere Clash
Body_Text:
Kampala — LIKE a thief expecting to get caught any time, Ayub Barasa glances over his shoulder as he hurriedly harvests rice from his garden in Namatala wetland.
He stuffs the harvest into a sisal bag he drags behind him, ready to flee at the first sign of danger. In his haste, he spills some of his precious harvest, ending up with more on the ground than in the bag. His face is covered in sweat, blinding his eyes, but he ignores it, just as he does the simmering midday sun, as he focuses on the task at hand. He can't ignore the nagging feeling that his life is in grave danger.

Original_Search_Title: Going Bananas to Fight Poverty And Hunger
Headline: Africa: Going Bananas to Fight Poverty And Hunger
Body_Text:
Mombasa — Arguably one of the world's most popular fruits, bananas are poorly marketed as a value-added commercial crop in Africa. But that is about to change as a plan is being conceptualised to transform the way Africa produces and sells bananas.
A five-day conference organised by the International Institute for Tropical Agriculture (IITA) and Biodiversity International, focussing on banana and plantain research throughout Africa, opened this week in Kenya's second largest city Mombasa. Plantains are a kind of banana.

Original_Search_Title: Predicting Weather With Science and Spider Webs
Headline: Kenya: Predicting Weather With Science and Spider Webs
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — When the magungu bird flies higher in the sky than usual and seems to float in the air in its passage from south to north, the Abasuba people living on the islands of Kenya's Lake Victoria and on the highlands near the lake know the rains are on their way and that it is time to plant.
To scientists, this flight pattern could be associated with the movement of the inter-tropical convergence zone. The Lake Victoria basin falls in an area of deep convection, which might explain the high flight.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Poor in Republic of Congo to Benefit From UN Grant
Headline: Congo-Brazzaville: Rural Poor in Republic of Congo to Benefit From UN Grant
Body_Text:
An $8.6 million grant to enhance agricultural production and provide food security for poor people in rural areas of the Republic of Congo was announced today by the United Nations International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD).
The initiative is part of a larger $18.7 million project in the Likouala, Pool and Sangha Departments, which aims to reach 250 villages and some 20,000 households, according to a news release issued by the Rome-based UN agency.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Partners Chinese Firm for $115 Million Ethanol Factory
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Partners Chinese Firm for $115 Million Ethanol Factory
Body_Text:
Jalingo — Taraba State government has signed an agreement with a Chinese firm, A-Dinota Ventures Ltd, to establish a $115 million ethanol factory in the state.
Addressing government officials during the signing of the agreement at the executive chamber of Government House, Jalingo, a representative of the Cassava Agro Industries Service, Mr Boma Anga, said the factory is an integrated ethanol plant that could produce 200,000 litres of fuel ethanol, 100 tons of cassava flour, 50 tons of liquid Co2 and 600,000 litres of bio fertilizers daily.

Original_Search_Title: Napep Disburses N122 Million to 101 Cooperatives in Cross River
Headline: Nigeria: Napep Disburses N122 Million to 101 Cooperatives in Cross River
Body_Text:
The National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAPEP) in Cross River State has disbursed a total of N121, 662,062 to about seven micro finance banks for onward disbursement to 101 cooperative societies across the 18 local councils of the state.
The money was disbursed under the NAPEP's Village Economic Development Solution programme, which is a local community-driven development programme. Under the programme, a community is expected to form cooperatives and thereafter is allowed to choose a project where it has comparative advantage while NAPEP would assist with the funding of the project through the cooperative.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Growers Association Board of Trustees Inaugurated
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Growers Association Board of Trustees Inaugurated
Body_Text:
The Cassava Growers Association of Nigeria (CGAN) yesterday inaugurated its new board of trustees in Gwagwalada Area Council.
An inaugural speech by the President of the association, Deacon B.S.B Adebayo, said the cassava stakeholders association was about technology developed method of cassava production, utilization, processing and marketing.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers, Hunters Threaten $50 Million ADB/World Bank Forestry Project in Ogun
Headline: Nigeria: Farmers, Hunters Threaten $50 Million ADB/World Bank Forestry Project in Ogun
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Activities of some illegal farmers and hunters are threatening the $50m African Development Bank (ADB)/World Bank assisted Area J4 Forest Reserve project located at Ijebu East local government of the state, the Ministry of Forestry disclosed on Monday.
The State's Commissioner, Alh. Ali Ajibode, who made the disclosure at a press briefing as part of the activities to mark the 2008 Forestry Week, said that a total of about 200,000 illegal farmers and hunters are degrading the entire OMO forest reserve in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Financial Crisis Can Open Doors for Poor Countries
Headline: South Africa: Financial Crisis Can Open Doors for Poor Countries
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — INFRASTRUCTURE can lead to development - but will it? A month bumping along the roads of Mozambique from Maputo to the Rovuma River provided the context for some ideas about public infrastructure and economic development and how both are financed.
Given that according to the news broadcasts capitalism was collapsing , the obvious question was what effect the financial crisis would have on poor countries such as Mozambique? And it occurred that from crisis could emerge an opportunity to restructure the global financial institutions in ways that would better serve poor countries in this difficult world.

Original_Search_Title: Green Revolution to Eliminate Grain Deficit
Headline: Mozambique: Green Revolution to Eliminate Grain Deficit
Body_Text:
Maputo — Government strategy towards agriculture is to eliminate the need to import rice and potatoes, and slash imports of wheat, declared Agriculture Minister Soares Nhaca on Wednesday.
Addressing the country's parliament, the Assembly of the Republic, in response to a request for information on food security from deputies of the ruling Frelimo Party, Nhaca said that, even before the sharp rise in world grain pries, the government "had a clear vision on the need to put food production and job creation at the top of our priorities".

Original_Search_Title: A Tribute to Dr. Kibirige Ssebunya
Headline: Uganda: A Tribute to Dr. Kibirige Ssebunya
Body_Text:
Kampala — DURING the burial of a village elder as told by Chinua Achebe in his best seller, Things Fall Apart, orator after orator came out and shouted their eulogies. Certainly, such a scenario would befit the late Dr. Israel Kibirige Ssebunya.
"If you were dull, I would ask the gods to make you active in death, but you were active," one of the orators would say.

Original_Search_Title: When the Petrodollar Cannot Save Locals From Food Insecurity
Headline: Nigeria: When the Petrodollar Cannot Save Locals From Food Insecurity
Body_Text:
The British coloniser robbed villages of the men who would have produced food while, buoyed by petrodollars, the generals silenced everybody - including farmers - into inactivity as the sector stagnated.
It has become a cliché to blame oil wealth for the food insecurity in Nigeria. This widely-held view ignores the destruction of the agricultural sector by the colonial and post-1966 military dictatorships.

Original_Search_Title: Does the Rain Season Prepare Farmers for Drought?
Headline: Rwanda: Does the Rain Season Prepare Farmers for Drought?
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwandan women growing beans under the shade of banana trees.
When you move around the countryside, you do not see people taking seriously the rain season we are enjoying. They are waiting to shed tears during the dry season when food is scarce and expensive.

Original_Search_Title: Country Takes Steps to Turn Bananas Into Cash Crop
Headline: Uganda: Country Takes Steps to Turn Bananas Into Cash Crop
Body_Text:
Kampala — Farmers, scientists and policy makers from around the world, are meeting in Mombasa to formulate a ten-year strategy expected to unlock the potential of banana as the next commercial crop to alleviate poverty and generate wealth.
The five-day conference, a first of its kind, comes at a time when banana is increasingly gaining importance as a cash crop and one mostly likely to lift rural households out of poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Green Belts to Boost Food Production
Headline: Malawi: Green Belts to Boost Food Production
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Malawian President Bingu wa Mutharika has pledged to embark on a "green belt" programme to enable the country, in the long run, to say goodbye to hunger and international food aid.
"Malawi appeals to the G-8 countries to support us to create a green belt around our lakes and along our rivers to irrigate land up to 20 kilometres from the shores. The Malawi government plans to grow a lot of rice, wheat, maize, millet, cassava, potatoes and beans for the local and international market," he told the United Nations General Assembly recently.

Original_Search_Title: Jigawa, Maradi States in Agric, Security Pact
Headline: Nigeria: Jigawa, Maradi States in Agric, Security Pact
Body_Text:
Dutse — An agreement has been reached between Jigawa State and Maradi State in Niger Republic to partner in agricultural development and security.
This was contained in a comminque signed by Governor Sule Lamido and his counterpart from Maradi, Alhaji Aliyu Muazu, which was made available to Daily Trust in Dutse. Under this agreement, the two governments agreed to cooperate on research and development of sorghum, cassava, cowpea, groundnut, millet and wheat. They would also share and exchange technology on animal husbandry and breeding.

Original_Search_Title: Rains Wreak Havoc
Headline: Rwanda: Rains Wreak Havoc
Body_Text:
Kigali — Torrential rains have caused extensive flooding, destroying homes and crops in Rwanda's western and northern regions, according to officials.
"The situation is pathetic; we are trying to secure relief aid for the affected people," Penelope Kantarama, governor of Western Province, said on 3 October. The rains submerged more than 500 homes, destroyed about 2,000 hectares of crops and washed away bridges, roads and pylons, as well as schools.

Original_Search_Title: Gulu Kids Battle Strange Disease
Headline: Uganda: Gulu Kids Battle Strange Disease
Body_Text:
Gulu — Barely three months after she first started feeling throbbing pains in her stomach, seven-year-old Stella Lalam's tummy has grown so big she can barely sit upright.
Every time she makes an effort to sit up, Lalam groans in obvious discomfort. Lalam's mother, Ms Sunday Lanyero, says she has spent numerous sleepless nights and lots of money in hospital but her daughter's condition has only continued to deteriorate.

Original_Search_Title: Imbalu Dancers Beat Up Host
Headline: Uganda: Imbalu Dancers Beat Up Host
Body_Text:
Kampala — A man is nursing serious injuries he sustained after a group of relatives beat him to near death for serving them 'substandard' food. Masolo of Bunangaka sub-county, Sironko hosted the Imbalu dancers for the last meal before his nephew could be circumcised.
As tradition demands, this final meal must be mouth-watering (chicken) in order to motivate the circumcision candidate into eating. Masolo, however, served his guests with dodo in groundnuts sauce and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Teenager Held for Raping Students
Headline: Uganda: Teenager Held for Raping Students
Body_Text:
Kampala — A teenager accused of raping Kyambogo University girls before robbing them of their property, is in Police custody.
The Police spokesperson, Judith Nabakooba, said yesterday that Kenneth Mwaka, 19, on Tuesday at 10:00pm went with two accomplices to a hostel near the university and raped three girls.

Original_Search_Title: Unchallenged Disparities - Gender and Socio-Spiritual Disparities During Ramadan
Headline: Tanzania: Unchallenged Disparities - Gender and Socio-Spiritual Disparities During Ramadan
Body_Text:
As Muslims globally come to the end of the holy month of Ramadan, Salma Maoulidi explores the continuing spiritual and secular inequalities experienced by Muslim Tanzanian women. Focussing on the gulf between spiritual goals and worldly reality, the absence of an effective redistributive alms system, and the differing realities faced by female and male followers, the author questions the extent to which a symbolic ritual of deprivation and sacrifice has been turned into a calculated wealth generating opportunity.
Millions of Muslims globally are nearing the last stretch of observing the obligatory fast that occurs each year during the ninth months of the Islamic Calendar, Ramadan. In the past most saw this time as a deeply spiritual time, a time in which to reconnect at the deepest level with the human state on the one hand and much higher spiritual state on the other through acts of physical discipline and spiritual meditation.

Original_Search_Title: Don't Heat-Up Polity, Politician Advises
Headline: Nigeria: Don't Heat-Up Polity, Politician Advises
Body_Text:
Ogun — A People's Democratic Party (PDP) leader in Ogun State, Otunba Idowu Odewale, has urged politicians to refrain from activities capable of heating up the polity.
Odewale gave the advise in a chat with newsmen in Abuja, adding that nobody or group should disrupt the peace that had paved the way for the general development witnessed in the last five years in the state.

Original_Search_Title: Ex-Gov Faults Policy Shift
Headline: Nigeria: Ex-Gov Faults Policy Shift
Body_Text:
Lagos — Former Gov. Abdullahi Adamu of Nasarawa State has criticised the tendency to abandon policies of previous administrations as a "national malaise".
Adamu, in an interview with the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja, said: " The problem is that we do not appear to build on our efforts over the years; there is no doubt about that.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Employ 4,000 Youths
Headline: Nigeria: Govt to Employ 4,000 Youths
Body_Text:
Governor Segun Oni of Ekiti State has said his administration plans to employ 4,000 youths into the public service.
He made the announcement yesterday in a broadcast to mark the 12th anniversary of the state.

Original_Search_Title: Wakiso; Where Marijuana is a Major Source of Income
Headline: Uganda: Wakiso; Where Marijuana is a Major Source of Income
Body_Text:
Growing Marijuana, commonly referred to as "weed" is illegal, but Sarah Tumwebaze and Andrew Bagala found out that some farmers in Wakiso District grow and sell it in broad daylight
As we waited at the Central Police Station (CPS) for the officers who were supposed to carry out the marijuana operation, three policemen brought in a man who seemed to be in his late twenties.

Original_Search_Title: Bad Weather Worsens Poverty in Kasese Region
Headline: Uganda: Bad Weather Worsens Poverty in Kasese Region
Body_Text:
Climate change is causing food scarcity in the Rwenzori region. Farmers there are no longer able to predict the seasons and so plan for the planting. They say that they are getting heavy rainfall with very strong winds, thunder, lightening and destructive hailstorms when expecting dry spells and that the effects have shortened the growing seasons for crops.
Human activities are to blame for the changes which have resulted in frequent floods and high temperatures. The change in the rainy seasons has troubled farmers who often lose their seeds because of continuous planting in expectation of favourable rainfall patterns.

Original_Search_Title: Don Advocates Alternative Energy
Headline: Nigeria: Don Advocates Alternative Energy
Body_Text:
Calabar — A don at the University of Calabar, Professor Okon Douglas Ekpa, has advocated the local production of bio-ethanol or bio-diesel as is currently being done America and Europe.
Ekpa made the call while delivering the 43rd Inaugural Lecture of the University of Calabar, Cross River State, said Nigeria has abundant raw materials from which these products could be produced. According to Ekpa, a Professor of Industrial Chemistry, bio-diesel could be produced from grains such as maize, sorghum, and millet as well as tubers such as cassava, yam, and cocoyam including sugarcane.

Original_Search_Title: Reflecting On Ways of Avoiding Destructions Wrought By Heavy Rain
Headline: Rwanda: Reflecting On Ways of Avoiding Destructions Wrought By Heavy Rain
Body_Text:
Kigali — Disaster struck several districts on Sunday. The afternoon heavy rains destroyed several homes in Rubavu, Kirehe and Ngoma districts in Rwanda's Eastern Province.
Resulting in a major humanitarian crisis; close to 2500 houses were destroyed in the three districts living hundreds of residents homeless.

Original_Search_Title: Agro Engineer Wins Second Presidential Science Award
Headline: Uganda: Agro Engineer Wins Second Presidential Science Award
Body_Text:
Kampala — PATIENCE pays, goes the adage, and this has been Joseph Kavuma's story - eking out a living from agro-processing machinery fabrication. "I still don't believe I won this year's Presidential Scientific Annual Award. It's great that my work has been recognised," says Kavuma.
The Minister of Communication and Information Technology, Ham Mulira, represented the President at the award-giving ceremony at the Kampala Serena Hotel recently.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens in Diaspora Seek U.S. Investment in Country
Headline: Nigeria: Citizens in Diaspora Seek U.S. Investment in Country
Body_Text:
Abuja — As Muslim faithful in Nigeria join their counterparts in other parts of the world to celebrate the end of fasting period (Ramadan), Nigerians in Diaspora have called for unity among all religious faithful in the country for the good of everyone.
The group also urged the government of the United States of America (USA) to invest part of the proposed $700billion aimed at bailing out the US economy in the agriculture sector of Nigeria through the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA) export.

Original_Search_Title: Tears of Joy As Ugandan Asians Visit Their Country
Headline: Uganda: Tears of Joy As Ugandan Asians Visit Their Country
Body_Text:
At 80 years, Mr Jethalal Pandya is no longer the sprightly businessman who took his first steps in business from Masaka District before the 1972 expulsion of the Asian community from Uganda by then president Idi Amin.
Mr Pandya cannot walk by himself anymore so he is moved from one place to another in a wheelchair.

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Destroy Hundreds of Homes
Headline: Rwanda: Heavy Rains Destroy Hundreds of Homes
Body_Text:
Kigali — Hundreds of houses were destroyed by heavy rains and storms in several parts of the country Sunday. The rains which started at about 5.30 pm in Rubavu district, Western Province left 35 people- among them 24 students who have been residing in different hostels in Gisenyi town,- admitted at Gisenyi Hospital, after suffering trauma.
Three of the students who were in critical condition were referred to Ruhengeri Hospital, medical officials said.
